title_en: Dubai Real Estate & Palm Jumeirah Visit
excerpt_en: Day 5 of the Dubai expedition. Today, I went to a Dubai developer's office to hear about Dubai real estate. I was genuinely curious about why the world's wealthy have their eyes on Dubai real estate. But since
body_en:
Day 5 of the Dubai expedition.

Today, I went to a Dubai developer's office to hear about Dubai real estate.

I was genuinely curious about why the world's wealthy have their eyes on Dubai real estate.

That said, it wasn't a high priority for me,

so I hadn't really looked into it on my own.

That's when I connected on social media with

a Japanese staff member at a local developer,

and they said we could meet at the office,

so I went the very next day (today).

It's important to keep some flexibility in your schedule!

When I arrived at the office,

I was shown to a huge meeting room,

and we had all sorts of conversations there.

About my story,

and about Dubai real estate too.

To get straight to the point,

if I were to invest in real estate,

Dubai would be my only choice.

There are many reasons behind that.

Why is Dubai real estate overwhelmingly popular?

First, Dubai is a place you can reach

from most countries within about 8 hours.

Being in the Middle East, it's accessible from both Europe and Asia.

So the access is great to begin with.

Furthermore, Dubai is not just a tourist destination or a real estate investment hub -- it's an international city that's competitive across multiple dimensions including business, taxation, infrastructure, and lifestyle.

From here, let me talk about Dubai's strengths.

1. Tax Advantages (Low Tax / Tax-Free System)

✅ Dubai's Tax System

  • No personal income tax (no taxation on salary or business income)
  • No capital gains tax (no tax on profits from selling stocks or real estate)
  • No property tax (low maintenance costs even when owning real estate)
  • No inheritance tax (assets can be passed on to the next generation as-is)
  • Corporate tax is 9% (however, profits under 375,000 AED per year have zero corporate tax)
  • Free zone companies enjoy zero corporate tax (with conditions)

⮕ Compared to other countries (Japan, Europe, America, etc.), the tax burden is overwhelmingly lower.

2. Global Business Hub

✅ Dubai's Business Environment

  • Geographically at the center of Europe, Asia, and Africa
  • Great access to Europe, Asia, and Africa.
  • *Has one of the world's largest hub airports (Dubai International Airport)** with excellent flight connections.
  • Free Zone (Special Economic Zone) system
  • Foreigners can establish 100%-owned companies (zero corporate tax within Free Zones).
  • This is one of the reasons major companies worldwide set up operations in Dubai.
  • Global talent converges here
  • With branches of multinational corporations gathered here, many talented foreign workers, investors, and entrepreneurs relocate here.
  • Not just wealthy people from Islamic countries, but capital from Europe, America, and Asia is concentrated here too.

⮕ Compared to Japan or Europe, it's easier to start a business and there's greater business freedom.

3. Advanced Infrastructure & Smart City

✅ Dubai's Appeal as a City of the Future

  • Ultra-high-rise buildings and cutting-edge urban design
  • The world's tallest building, Burj Khalifa
  • Artificial island, Palm Jumeirah
  • Future city concepts: Dubai Harbour, Dubai Creek
  • Promoting smart city initiatives
  • Using blockchain technology, administrative procedures are fully digitized.
  • Paper documents have been abolished, with a government system that handles everything digitally.
  • In 2023, they announced a "Metaverse Strategy," focusing on the virtual economy as well.
  • Developed transportation infrastructure
  • *Dubai Metro (driverless railway)** supports intra-city travel.
  • One of the world's largest international airports (Emirates Airlines' hub).
  • Hyperloop plan (a concept to travel between Abu Dhabi and Dubai in 12 minutes).

⮕ In many ways more advanced than Japan's urban planning, with strong appeal as a cutting-edge smart city.

4. Excellent Public Safety

✅ One of the Safest Cities in the World

  • Extremely low crime rate
  • Being an Islamic region with strict laws, pickpocketing, fraud, and violent crimes are rare.
  • Ranks high in "World's Safest City Rankings."
  • A foreigner-friendly environment
  • With a multinational population, cultural diversity is embraced.
  • Alcohol is also legal in certain areas (unusual for an Islamic region).

⮕ Compared to cities in America or Europe, the sense of safety is overwhelmingly higher.

5. Lifestyle for the Wealthy & High-Income Earners

✅ Dubai's Unique Luxury Life

  • Ultra-luxury hotels & resorts
  • Burj Al Arab (the world's only 7-star hotel)
  • Atlantis, The Palm (luxury resort)
  • Relocation leveraging tax benefits
  • More and more European wealthy individuals are relocating to Dubai for tax purposes.
  • Its role as a "tax haven" is also strengthening.
  • Sports & entertainment are also well-developed
  • F1, golf, tennis, and other international events are held frequently
  • One of the world's largest shopping malls (Dubai Mall)

⮕ Even compared to Singapore and Hong Kong, Dubai is an attractive relocation destination for high-income earners.

6. Cryptocurrency, Finance & Investment Environment

✅ Appeal as a Financial Market

  • Cryptocurrency & blockchain sectors are thriving
  • Since 2023, cryptocurrency regulations have been relaxed, with efforts to attract exchanges and companies.
  • Global Web3 and DeFi projects are moving their bases to Dubai.
  • Easy international remittances
  • Combined with tax benefits, there is a high degree of freedom in moving funds.
  • Compared to Japan and Europe, the cryptocurrency and fintech sectors are more active.

⮕ For those interested in cryptocurrency and digital asset investment, Dubai is becoming one of the world's top hubs.

So as you can see,

most cutting-edge technology companies

have their bases in Dubai,

and for someone like me who deals with crypto assets, it's overwhelmingly easy to benefit from this, and

from a tax perspective, it's overwhelmingly favorable too.

Also, here's

the reason I think Dubai is the only choice.

Fundamentally, the Dubai government has strict regulations on real estate development, with systems in place to protect investors' funds.

As a result, when purchasing pre-construction real estate (off-plan properties), the risk of developers running off with investors' money is greatly reduced.

Dubai's Real Estate Regulations and Fund Management Systems

  1. Escrow System
  • In Dubai, real estate fund management through escrow accounts (trust accounts) is mandatory.
  • Funds paid by investors do not go directly to the developer but are deposited into government-designated escrow accounts.
  • Developers can withdraw funds in stages according to project progress (e.g., upon completion of foundation work, building structure, etc.).
  1. Oversight by RERA (Real Estate Regulatory Agency)
  • Dubai's real estate market is managed by RERA (Real Estate Regulatory Agency) under the Dubai Land Department (DLD).
  • Only developers registered with RERA can sell projects.
  • If planned construction doesn't progress, RERA intervenes to protect investors' funds.
  1. Project Registration and Guarantees
  • Developers must pay a guarantee deposit to the government before starting a project (typically 20-30% of construction costs).
  • There are regulations that prevent sales until a certain level of construction progress is confirmed, to prevent projects from falling through.
  1. Fraud Risk Reduction
  • In the past, during the real estate bubble (around 2008), there were cases of developers going bankrupt after collecting funds, but regulations have been strengthened since then.
  • It is illegal for unlicensed developers to sell off-plan properties, and government oversight is strict.

Conclusion: The Risk of Developer Fraud in Dubai is Extremely Low

  • Fund management through escrow accounts
  • Strict oversight by the government (DLD & RERA)
  • Developer registration system
  • Payment of funds according to construction progress

Thanks to these systems, investors' funds are protected, and the risk of developers absconding with money is nearly zero. Therefore, off-plan real estate investment is safer compared to other countries.

In other words, the Dubai government

has introduced systems to protect investors,

and management is so thorough that you can even check online how far along the construction is.

On top of that, Dubai is going to

develop even further,

and apparently a casino will open by 2027.

By the way,

the construction cost of the Burj Khalifa was

$1.5 billion USD (about 135 billion yen),

and the upcoming casino resort

is said to exceed $3 billion USD.

That's insane, haha.

For these reasons,

I found Dubai real estate really interesting.

Also, this office had a showroom attached, and I was given a tour.

Beautiful.

Apparently there was a property going on sale the following week,

and the office was packed with investors.

It was a scene you wouldn't easily see in Japan.

On a normal Dubai sightseeing trip,

you wouldn't go all the way to a developer's office

to hear their story,

but I enjoy this kind of approach,

and hearing things I didn't know is really interesting.

I also talked about masu,

so I thought it would be amazing if we could do something combining Dubai real estate and masu.

Anyway, it was an incredibly educational experience.

🔻 To Palm Jumeirah

Palm Jumeirah** is an artificial island in Dubai, United Arab Emirates (UAE), and one of the world's most famous landmarks.

1. Basic Information

  • Location: Dubai, United Arab Emirates (UAE)
  • Construction started: 2001
  • Developer: Nakheel
  • Shape: Designed to resemble a palm tree
  • Uses: Luxury residences, hotels, resorts, shopping, entertainment facilities, etc.

2. Features

  • One of the world's largest artificial islands, created by reclaiming the sea.
  • *Consists of three areas: "Trunk," "Fronds," and "Crescent."**
  • Trunk: The main street, lined with many commercial facilities and luxury apartments.
  • Fronds: Luxury residential area with villas and private beaches.
  • Crescent: Located on the outer perimeter, lined with luxurious resort hotels and also serving as a breakwater.

This time, I went to the observation deck and viewed it from above.

Photos don't really do it justice,

but in person, it's truly amazing.

There were

lots of tourists here,

but the crowd was a bit different from other tourist spots.

What I mean is, wealthy people and influencers were gathered here.

Plus people who aspire to be celebrities.

In other words, both the real deal and the fakes were present. Haha.

A lot of people were also intentionally wearing flashy, stylish outfits -- this isn't as noticeable in other countries, haha.

In other words, it's a gathering place for people

who want to post photos on Instagram.

There was a lounge and cafe here,

with incredibly expensive items for sale.

Personally, what I saw as a business opportunity was the medal vending machine at the observation deck.

There are two types,

but they look kind of beat-up, haha.

If I could hypothetically place

MASUKAME capsule toy machines here,

even if just 10% of visitors played the gacha,

it would generate significant profits.

Celebrities and influencers from around the world,

plus people who pretend to be, all gather here --

people who want to attract attention in any way possible,

so if you could place exciting content here, it would have a massive impact. And

this is the ultimate daydream, but

if MASUKAME capsule toy machines could be set up

at both the Palm Jumeirah observation deck

and the Burj Khalifa observation deck,

I think we could do something really incredible.

Capsule toy machines overseas are still pretty beat-up,

so this kind of technology should be imported from Japan,

and if the character designs were created with a designer well-versed in Japanese anime and manga culture, together with a Dubai developer, I think it would be incredibly powerful.

And at FOMUS, we're already implementing new forms of traditional crafts utilizing blockchain technology,

so I don't think there's anything else that could create such synergy,

and even if you used existing characters,

people wouldn't go out of their way to play a capsule machine for them,

but if it were a joint character between Dubai and Japan,

that's a different story entirely,

and since there is no Dubai-original character yet,

having Japanese professional designers involved to ensure quality, and teaming up with Dubai's real estate team, would be incredibly powerful.

I had been thinking about

trying to secure this position in Europe,

but expanding in Dubai first seems more interesting,

and from there, I'm excited about branching out to other countries.

I have the drive to execute this idea, so if I can just manage to gather Dubai investors, all sorts of things become possible, and

if this works out, I could use that money to

buy real estate in Dubai,

and set it up so that FOMUS members can stay there when they visit Dubai.

This way, from the Dubai government's perspective,

it would benefit them more to have FOMUS be active,

because ultimately that money flows into Dubai,

so it's a positive for them.

Earn in Dubai, spend in Dubai.

Through this European expedition,

the capsule toy idea was born,

and I learned that by using stickers for masu designs, the color limitation problem goes away --

these were two major learnings and realizations.

And in this Dubai expedition,

I realized that if you place them where wealthy people and influencers gather,

everyone will naturally share about it, so I think we can gain tremendous influence.

And this is something that can only be done because a Japanese business owner and a Dubai business owner team up, so it needs to move forward with Dubai investors on the team.

This is such an exciting development!!

Of course, this is still at the concept stage.

But I don't feel like I can lose.

I've been able to articulate this much,

so now I just need to refine the details a bit more

and pitch to Dubai investors.

Coming to Palm Jumeirah was absolutely the right call.

*A guy going solo to an observation deck isn't necessary for sightseeing, but going up was definitely the right call.

After that, I headed to Atlantis at the far end of Jumeirah.

Here, hotel guests have access to all the facilities, so I just strolled around the areas that were free to enter.

It's a complete resort area, so

if we could get masu placed in spots like these, that would be amazing, and

the difficulty level feels lower than

trying to break into other European countries.

Also, you get around by monorail,

and the branching areas of Palm Jumeirah

still have tons of unfinished spots.

It became vividly clear that

this place is going to develop even further.

🔻 Dinner

For dinner, I went with my two hosts

to a restaurant near Palm Jumeirah.

Since I was in the area,

they came to pick me up on their way

and took me to a restaurant they recommended.

We had Georgian and Kazakhstani food, and everything was incredibly delicious.

After that,

we met up with their friends

and enjoyed hanging out.

One of those two friends

was a flight attendant for Emirates,

and the other was in the wholesale snack business.

Everyone's doing interesting things,

so chatting with them is really fun.

And for some reason, we ended up playing UNO, haha.

"When in Rome, do as the Romans do" -- and sure enough,

the rules were different from the Japanese rules,

but I followed them perfectly, haha.

By the way,

before meeting up,

I bought some Karak Chai.

This is

a sweet, spiced milk tea.

It's loaded with sugar and condensed milk, giving it a rich sweetness.

It's fragrant with cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, and other spices.

It's commonly enjoyed at local cafes in Dubai and the UAE.

Since I'd never had it before,

the two of them recommended it to me.

It was incredibly delicious.

So that's how

I spent a wonderful time.

Through Couchsurfing,

making these new connections,

my first Dubai adventure has been incredibly fulfilling.

excerpt_en: So today I had no personal plans, and Kareem, my Couchsurfing host, had some business in "Sharjah" and offered to show me around a bit while he was there, so I went! Sharjah
body_en:
So today I had no personal plans,

and Kareem, my Couchsurfing host, had some business in "Sharjah" and offered to show me around a bit while he was there,

so I went!

Sharjah is one of the seven emirates that make up the United Arab Emirates (UAE), and it's a region that's especially highly regarded for its culture, arts, and history.

Sharjah is located in the northeastern part of the UAE, right next to Dubai. It has a large population and plays an important role as a center of regional economy and culture.

This area is basically where Arab and Asian people live, and there's no luxury feel whatsoever.

This region is a bit far from Dubai, and the metro doesn't connect there,

so basically the only access is by bus or private car.

First, to take care of Kareem's business,

we went to a car repair shop.

There are still quite a few Japanese cars in Dubai,

and the one we were riding in was a Nissan.

His errand took about 20 minutes,

and then he showed me around.

This is Sharjah.

It's similar to Old Dubai,

but it's not a tourist area,

so to put it simply, it's like a bedroom community for Dubai.

Work in Dubai. Live in Sharjah.

That's the vibe.

Since it's Ramadan,

the restaurants in Sharjah are closed.

This is one area where it differs from Dubai.

*Dubai, with its many tourists, operates normally.

Also, since everyone heads home to Sharjah after work, there's traffic even on three-lane roads.

In the morning, the Dubai-bound direction is the one that gets congested.

Also, I'd had a lingering question about

why the metro is always so packed --

it's because people from Sharjah

take buses to metro stations,

then ride the metro to work in Dubai,

which is why it's always crowded.

Dubai has rapidly flourished as a tourist destination,

and while the capital Abu Dhabi is one thing,

Sharjah has a very strong presence

as a bedroom community.

If I were to live here, Dubai would be my only choice.

🔻 Cooking Preparations

Today is my 3rd day staying at this house.

It's my last night here,

and for the first time, we ate together at their place.

There was very little I could help with,

so the three of us shelled green peas, haha.

That's about all I could contribute.

The rest I left to them.

Since they're an Algerian and Italian couple,

dishes from both countries were served.

First, Caprese.

And then...

A dish commonly eaten in North Africa, especially in the Maghreb region of Algeria and Tunisia.

This is a type of traditional Algerian soup called "Berboukes" or "Alicha."

  • It contains small pearl-shaped pasta (berboukes) or couscous, in a soup made with chickpeas, tomatoes, and spices.

This is like a drinkable yogurt.

It's a fermented milk drink commonly consumed in the Middle East and North Africa (often called "laban" or "labneh").

  • The one on the left, "Soummam L'Ben," is a fermented milk drink produced by an Algerian brand. As the French label "L'Ben" indicates, it's a type of drink called "leben (labun)" that's common in North Africa.
  • The one on the right, "Almarai Fresh Laban," is a fermented milk drink from "Almarai," a brand commonly seen in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf countries.

And then,

This is a type of "Couscous."

Couscous is a granular pasta made from durum wheat semolina, and it's a signature dish of North Africa.

It had green peas and fava beans in it --

a healthy dish that was quite delicious.

I forgot to take a photo, but

they also prepared a curry-spiced soup,

and eating it together with the couscous was delicious.

By the way,

everyone was pouring this laban over their couscous to eat.

Food culture really is completely different.

After that, cake!

And also hazelnut cream.

This is apparently also from Algeria.

Properly delicious.

Their Kazakhstani friend also joined us for the meal.

If I hadn't been using Couchsurfing,

I never would have had an experience like this, and

I'm truly grateful to them.

On top of that, it wasn't actually me who sent the request --

I had posted

"I'm going to Dubai, so please contact me if you can host me!"

and they read it and

sent me a message. That's how it started.

Staying at a hotel is fine too,

but being able to stay at someone's actual home

is truly something to be thankful for,

and they drove me around too --

I was indebted to them for the entire 3 days.

When they come to Japan,

I'll give them the full hospitality treatment.

10 days in Dubai is too long for just sightseeing,

so being able to have time for exchanges like this is really great!
